questions.question: Define a priority queue. How does it differ from a regular FIFO queue in terms of element ordering?
keyPoints: Priority queue is an ADT where each element has an associated priority value; element with highest priority is dequeued first regardless of insertion order
Regular queue follows FIFO order strictly - elements are served based on arrival time, not priority
In priority queue, insertion order does not matter; priority determines deletion order

questions.question: Explain ascending priority queue (min-PQ) and descending priority queue (max-PQ) with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Ascending priority queue (min-PQ): element with smallest priority value has highest priority and is served first using deleteMin() operation
Descending priority queue (max-PQ): element with largest priority value has highest priority and is served first using deleteMax() operation
Example min-PQ: Insert (A,5), (B,1), (C,3) → Delete order: B(1)→C(3)→A(5); used in Dijkstra's, Prim's, Huffman coding
Example max-PQ: Insert (A,5), (B,1), (C,3) → Delete order: A(5)→C(3)→B(1); used in CPU scheduling, emergency systems
Choice depends on application semantics: lower values mean higher priority (min-PQ) or higher values mean higher priority (max-PQ)

questions.question: Compare unsorted array and sorted array implementations of priority queues. Explain the time complexity of insert and deleteMin operations for each.
keyPoints: Unsorted array: Insert places element at end in O(1); DeleteMin requires scanning entire array O(n) to find minimum
Sorted array: Insert requires finding position and shifting elements O(n); DeleteMin is O(1) from end
Sorted array trick: For min-PQ, store elements in descending order of priority so smallest is at end for O(1) deletion
Trade-off: Unsorted array preferred when insertions > deletions; sorted array preferred when deletions > insertions
Both implementations use O(n) space

questions.question: Implement a priority queue using a sorted linked list. Write C functions for insert and deleteMin operations and analyze their time complexities.
keyPoints: Node structure: struct pqNode { int data; int priority; struct pqNode *next; }
Insert operation: traverse list to find correct position maintaining ascending priority order - O(n) time
DeleteMin operation: remove head node (smallest priority) - O(1) time
Handle edge cases: empty list, insertion at head, insertion at tail
Trade-off compared to unsorted list: fast deletion O(1) but slow insertion O(n)

questions.question: Compare the time complexities of the following priority queue implementations: unsorted array, sorted array, sorted linked list, and binary heap. Which implementation is preferred for general-purpose use and why?
keyPoints: Unsorted array: Insert O(1), DeleteMin O(n), Peek O(n)
Sorted array: Insert O(n), DeleteMin O(1), Peek O(1)
Sorted linked list: Insert O(n), DeleteMin O(1), Peek O(1)
Binary heap: Insert O(log n), DeleteMin O(log n), Peek O(1)
Binary heap is preferred: provides O(log n) for both insert and delete, which is optimal for comparison-based structures
Build time: Binary heap achieves O(n) via heapify, better than sorted array O(n log n)
